title: Audit agent tool usage as primary product metric, not just recall benchmarks
context: Memory products measure recall benchmarks, but the real question is whether an agent reaches for the right tool at the right moment during actual work, or falls back to grep and manual search.
decision: Conduct agent self-audits of tool usage during real feature work, treat each finding as a concrete product bug (traced to a decision ID), institute golden-set evaluation gating all retrieval changes in CI, and publish the audit results as the canonical measure of product success.
consequences: Discovered 4 concrete entry-point failures that showed up only in real work (not benchmarks): grounding tax, empty task-shaped queries, ranking misses, overly broad blocking rules
Shifted changelog from version notes to decision-ID-traced claims (every statement links to a committed graph decision)
Exposed golden-set rot problem: test expectations become stale same way as documentation, requiring supersession tracking
Reduced grounding injection cost ~100× by deduplication, enabling agents to see recent decisions + roadmap at session open
Achieved Recall@10 90% → 100% on golden set with bounded-floor ranking that dims history without losing it
Created self-emptying roadmap: findings become proposed decisions, proposed decisions surface on roadmap, shipped fixes remove them
alternatives_rejected: Measuring only recall benchmarks like other memory products — doesn't reveal if agent actually uses tools when it matters
Treating audit findings as nice-to-have polish — decided findings are blockers, shipped all fixes before v1.3.0
Keeping agent audits and decision graph internal — decided to publish findings and commit graph openly to prove the loop works

rejected_tripwires.alternative: Measuring only recall benchmarks like other memory products — doesn't reveal if agent actually uses tools when it matters
Treating audit findings as nice-to-have polish — decided findings are blockers, shipped all fixes before v1.3.0
Keeping agent audits and decision graph internal — decided to publish findings and commit graph openly to prove the loop works
